title: Two Sum
description:
Given an array of integers `nums` and an integer `target`, return indices of the two numbers such that they add up to `target`.

You may assume that each input would have exactly one solution, and you may not use the same element twice.

You can return the answer in any order.
whatShouldHappen: Find two numbers that add up to the target
Return their indices as an array
skeletonHint: Use a Map to store the numbers you have seen so far and their indices. For each number, calculate its complement (target - num) and check if it exists in the Map.
patternExplanation: By using a Hash Map, we can trade space for time, reducing the lookup time for the complement from O(n) to O(1).

title: Contains Duplicate
description: Given an integer array `nums`, return `true` if any value appears at least twice in the array, and return `false` if every element is distinct.
whatShouldHappen: Check if any element is repeated
Return true if duplicates exist, false otherwise
skeletonHint: Use a Set to keep track of unique elements. If you encounter an element already in the Set, a duplicate is found.
patternExplanation: A Hash Set allows for O(1) average time complexity for both insertions and lookups, making it efficient for detecting duplicates.

title: Container With Most Water
description:
You are given an integer array `height` of length `n`. There are `n` vertical lines drawn such that the two endpoints of the `i-th` line are `(i, 0)` and `(i, height[i])`.

Find two lines that together with the x-axis form a container, such that the container contains the most water.

Return the maximum amount of water a container can store.
whatShouldHappen: Calculate the area between two lines: `min(h1, h2) * distance`
Find the pair of lines that yields the maximum area
Optimize to avoid a nested loop approach
skeletonHint: Use two pointers, one at each end of the array. The area is limited by the shorter line. To potentially find a larger area, move the pointer pointing to the shorter line inward.
patternExplanation: The two-pointer approach allows us to explore potential containers from widest to narrowest, greedily discarding lines that can no longer contribute to a larger area.

title: 3Sum
description:
Given an integer array nums, return all the triplets `[nums[i], nums[j], nums[k]]` such that `i != j`, `i != k`, and `j != k`, and `nums[i] + nums[j] + nums[k] == 0`.

Notice that the solution set must not contain duplicate triplets.
whatShouldHappen: Sort the array to handle duplicates and use two pointers
Find all unique triplets that sum to zero
Skip duplicate elements to ensure unique results
skeletonHint: Sort the input array. Iterate through the array with a fixed element `nums[i]`, and use two pointers (`left` and `right`) to find pairs that sum to `-nums[i]`. Remember to skip duplicate values for `i`, `left`, and `right`.
patternExplanation: Sorting combined with the two-pointer approach allows us to efficiently find triplets while avoiding duplicate combinations in O(n²) time.

title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
description:
There is an integer array `nums` sorted in ascending order (with distinct values).

Prior to being passed to your function, `nums` is **possibly rotated** at an unknown pivot index `k` (`1 <= k < nums.length`) such that the resulting array is `[nums[k], nums[k+1], ..., nums[n-1], nums[0], nums[1], ..., nums[k-1]]`.

Given the array `nums` **after** the possible rotation and an integer `target`, return *the index of target if it is in nums, or -1 if it is not in nums*.

You must write an algorithm with `O(log n)` runtime complexity.
whatShouldHappen: Determine which half of the array is sorted
Check if the target lies within the sorted half
Maintain O(log n) time complexity
skeletonHint: Even if the array is rotated, one half of the array (relative to the midpoint) will always be sorted. Identify which half is sorted, and then check if the target could be in that range.
patternExplanation: Modified binary search allows us to find the target in O(log n) time by exploiting the fact that at least one half of any subarray is still sorted.

title: Combination Sum
description:
Given an array of **distinct** integers `candidates` and a target integer `target`, return a list of all **unique combinations** of `candidates` where the chosen numbers sum to `target`. You may return the combinations in **any order**.

The **same** number may be chosen from `candidates` an **unlimited number of times**. Two combinations are unique if the frequency of at least one of the chosen numbers is different.

The test cases are generated such that the number of unique combinations that sum up to `target` is less than `150` combinations for the given input.
whatShouldHappen: Explore all possible combinations using recursion
Allow the same number to be reused multiple times
Backtrack when the current sum exceeds the target
skeletonHint: Use a backtracking function that takes the remaining target, the current starting index in candidates, and the current combination built so far. If remaining is 0, you found a valid combination. If remaining < 0, stop exploring that path.
patternExplanation: Backtracking is used to systematically explore all combinations by adding a candidate, recursing, and then 'undoing' the choice to try the next candidate.

title: Maximum Subarray
description:
Given an integer array `nums`, find the subarray with the largest sum, and return its sum.

A **subarray** is a contiguous non-empty sequence of elements within an array.
whatShouldHappen: Identify the contiguous subarray that yields the highest sum
Return the sum of this subarray
Use Kadane's Algorithm for an O(n) solution
skeletonHint: At each position, decide whether to start a new subarray with the current element or extend the existing subarray. Keep track of the maximum sum seen so far.
patternExplanation: Kadane's Algorithm is a dynamic programming approach that finds the maximum subarray sum in linear time by making a local optimal choice at each step.

title: Jump Game
description:
You are given an integer array `nums`. You are initially positioned at the array's **first index**, and each element in the array represents your maximum jump length at that position.

Return `true` if you can reach the last index, or `false` otherwise.
whatShouldHappen: Determine if the end of the array is reachable from the start
Track the maximum reachable index as you iterate through the array
Return false if you reach an index that is further than your maximum reachable index
skeletonHint: Keep track of the furthest index you can reach (`maxReach`). Iterate through the array; if your current index `i` is greater than `maxReach`, it means you can't reach this index, so return false. Otherwise, update `maxReach` to `max(maxReach, i + nums[i])`.
patternExplanation: A greedy approach works here because at each step we only care about the maximum reach we can achieve, which determines if we can eventually reach the end.

title: Maximum Product Subarray
description:
Given an integer array `nums`, find a subarray that has the largest product, and return the product.

The test cases are generated so that the answer will fit in a **32-bit** integer.
whatShouldHappen: Keep track of both maximum and minimum products ending at each position
Handle negative numbers which can flip a minimum product into a maximum
Reset the current product if a zero is encountered
skeletonHint: Use dynamic programming to track the maximum and minimum products up to the current element. When you multiply by a negative number, the maximum becomes the minimum and vice versa.
patternExplanation: We maintain both the maximum and minimum product ending at the current position because a negative number can turn a very small minimum into a very large maximum.

title: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array
description:
Suppose an array of length `n` sorted in ascending order is **rotated** between `1` and `n` times. For example, the array `nums = [0,1,2,4,5,6,7]` might become:

* `[4,5,6,7,0,1,2]` if it was rotated `4` times.
* `[0,1,2,4,5,6,7]` if it was rotated `7` times.

Notice that rotating an array `[a[0], a[1], a[2], ..., a[n-1]]` 1 time results in the array `[a[n-1], a[0], a[1], a[2], ..., a[n-2]]`.

Given the sorted rotated array `nums` of **unique** elements, return *the minimum element of this array*.

You must write an algorithm that runs in `O(log n) time`.
whatShouldHappen: Find the pivot point where the array rotation occurs
Use binary search to achieve O(log n) complexity
Identify which side of the midpoint contains the minimum
skeletonHint: Use binary search. At each step, compare `nums[mid]` with `nums[right]`. If `nums[mid] > nums[right]`, the minimum must be in the right half. Otherwise, it's in the left half (including `mid`).
patternExplanation: Binary search works here because the array is still somewhat sorted. By comparing the middle element with the rightmost element, we can consistently discard half of the array.

title: Longest Consecutive Sequence
description:
Given an unsorted array of integers `nums`, return *the length of the longest consecutive elements sequence*.

You must write an algorithm that runs in `O(n)` time.
whatShouldHappen: Identify the start of a consecutive sequence
Count the length of each sequence starting from its first element
Return the maximum sequence length found
skeletonHint: Use a Set for O(1) lookups. For each number `n`, check if `n - 1` exists. If not, `n` is the start of a potential sequence. Use a loop to find how many consecutive numbers follow `n`.
patternExplanation: By storing all numbers in a Hash Set, we can check for the existence of the start of a sequence (num - 1) in O(1) time, allowing us to build sequences efficiently.

title: Product of Array Except Self
description:
Given an integer array `nums`, return an array `answer` such that `answer[i]` is equal to the product of all the elements of `nums` except `nums[i]`.

The product of any prefix or suffix of `nums` is guaranteed to fit in a 32-bit integer.

You must write an algorithm that runs in `O(n)` time and without using the division operation.
whatShouldHappen: The output array must have the same length as the input array.
Each element at index `i` must be the product of all elements except `nums[i]`.
The algorithm should not use the division operator.
skeletonHint: Consider using two passes: one to calculate products of elements to the left, and another to multiply by products of elements to the right.
patternExplanation: By precalculating the product of all elements to the left (prefix) and all elements to the right (suffix) of each index, we can find the total product except self in O(n) time.
